evidence.page_title: Are German Police Wearing Chainmail to Defend Against Refugee ...
evidence.title: Are German Police Wearing Chainmail to Defend Against Refugee Attacks?
evidence.description: Photographs show members of German special forces wearing chainmail-like protective gear, but not because of an increase in refugee stabbing attacks.
evidence.text:
Photographs show German special forces officers wearing chainmail-like protective gear in preparation for specific instances of dealing with attackers armed with stabbing or cutting weapons.
This protective gear was not introduced in 2016, is not worn by all German police officers, and was not adopted to deal with an increase in stabbing attacks by refugees.
In September 2016, photographs purportedly showing German police officers wearing chainmail-like protective gear were circulated on social media, along with the claim that the retro form or armo had been made part of the official everyday uniform due to an increase in knife attacks by refugees:
Although the above-displayed photographs are real, they do not show the everyday attire of German police officers, nor do they show a uniform change that resulted from an increase in refugee stabbing attacks.
The pictured protective gear seen here is occasionally worn by German special forces, Spezialeinsatzkommandos (SEK), when they are heading into a situation that involves someone armed with a sharp stabbing or cutting weapon. For instance, the man pictured on the right in the image above was tracking down an individual armed with a pruning saw who had evaded police in Minden, Germany, on 19 September 2016.
We traced the second image back to an August 2015 post on a German message board entitled "Fuerzas de Elite (Elite Forces)," where users claimed that it was taken in Frankfurt, Germany, as police responded to an incident of a man threatening to kill himself and his wife with a knife.

evidence.title: chain mail
evidence.description: Video explaining the manufacturing process of chain armor This i
evidence.text:
chain mail
Last Modified:
1. outline
It is an armor in the form of a chain woven with rings made of wire . It is a representative iron armor that has been used for a long time in various countries since ancient times .[2] In Korean , it is expressed as chain armor or chain armor. In the Chinese character expression for 'Swaejagap', the word Seja (鎖子) means a chain.
2. etymology
In modern times, the expression 'chain mail ' is common, but in fact, the English word called Chain-Mail is a coined word designed in the 18th century, and it is like a jokbal . The word mail means 'armor made of chain', so it was just called mail. In English, mail is unconditionally chain armor or relatives.
English's mail is a word that Latin Macula has been changed to MAILLE through Italian Maglia and has been settled in Mail, Mayle, or just chain. In the Roman era, this armor was called Macula, and the Normans used the expression net, like 'His War-Net, Woven by Cunning of Smith'.
The reason for using the expression chain-mail today is that in modern English, the alumni , which means mail, have overwhelmingly increase the use of the word mail. There was no mail in the days when the chain armor was widely used, and after the mail system was created, there was no confusion because the chain armor disappeared. Therefore, when referring to chain armor today, it is to add chain to reveal that it is not mail. In other words, the word Chain-mail has never been used.
For this reason, modern historians and downgraded researchers use the French word MAILLE more than the English word mail.
English's mail is a word that Latin Macula has been changed to MAILLE through Italian Maglia and has been settled in Mail, Mayle, or just chain. In the Roman era, this armor was called Macula, and the Normans used the expression net, like 'His War-Net, Woven by Cunning of Smith'.
The reason for using the expression chain-mail today is that in modern English, the alumni , which means mail, have overwhelmingly increase the use of the word mail. There was no mail in the days when the chain armor was widely used, and after the mail system was created, there was no confusion because the chain armor disappeared. Therefore, when referring to chain armor today, it is to add chain to reveal that it is not mail. In other words, the word Chain-mail has never been used.
For this reason, modern historians and downgraded researchers use the French word MAILLE more than the English word mail.
3. history
Chainmail armor is thought to have been first made by the Celts of ancient Europe . The oldest chainmail armor that remains today is the Gauls excavated in Eastern Europe or the Etruscans excavated in Italy, and scholars estimate that it dates back to around the 3rd or 4th century BC. Afterwards, it spread throughout Europe, North Africa, West Asia, Central Asia, India, etc., and it seems that chainmail was introduced to East Asia through Central Asia around the 8th century.
3.1. use
3.1.1. europe
European armor is largely divided into so-called 'traditional' armor made of bronze and iron in ancient Greece and ancient Rome , and those made of leather and chains made by Rome's enemies, 'barbarians'.
Armor developed in Greece and Rome, the foundation of European culture, did not directly affect medieval Europe, and later mimics the form of Roman era in later Renaissance, but the manufacturing technology and form were not transferred. Rather, Rome began to use the chain armor used by the enemies of Rome, and it continued to assess the Middle Ages.
First of all, among the current relics, the chain armor of the Gauls or Etruscans of the 3rd and 4th centuries BC is presumed to be the oldest. There are some sources that suggest that chains were also used in Greece, and in Egypt . Chain-like objects are also seen in pictures of pharaohs ' costumes, and it is possible that the Sarmatians and Phoenicians also wore them, but no clear artifacts have been found.
What is certain is that chain mail appeared after the Iron Age, and that the Bronze Age armies, like the armies of pre-19th Dynasty Egyptians, did not wear chain mail. If bronze is made into chain armor, the chain may bend or break due to the ductility of bronze. In order for the chain to not break easily, it must be made of a strong metal material such as iron, so bronze armor during the Bronze Age was mostly made of plate, bronze plates were added to leather, or small bronze plates were woven with string to form leather armor or armor. wore it Therefore, there is no possibility that the pharaohs of the previous Egyptian dynasties, except after the Nubian dynasty, wore chain armor.
The Romans used chain armor during battles with the Celts in what is now northern Italy starting around the 1st century BC. Following the Roman tradition of accepting anything useful, they adopted the chain armor of the Celts in the form of lorica hamata . Riveting rings have already been used since the days of Lorica Hamata, and one set was produced using about 30,000 standard steel rings with an inner diameter of 5 mm and an outer diameter of 7 mm. The lorica hamata was widely used as the standard armor of the Roman army for a long time until the military reform in the late 3rd century .[3]
And at this time, the preached chain armor was used in Europe until the 13th century.[4] In fact, it was used by the 4th -century Goths, the 12th century crusaders, or the basic structure, but there were detailed differences depending on the times.
3.1.2. East Asia
Three Kingdoms in Korean history The record of the first chain armor is about the record that Dang Tae -jong was in the shrine of Dongmyeongseong in Yodongseong when he played Goguryeo . However, there is no way to check whether the armor, which was in the same name, was really used by the king of the same name, and whether the chain armor we thought was right. There are many similar records worldwide, and there are no similar relics discovered on Manchuria or the Korean peninsula . It is from the Goryeo Dynasty to use the truth -shaped armor on the Korean peninsula. In Korea , it was known that the iron plate was cut and weaved with a string , and it was known that the chain armor was not used well, but in the end of Goryeo and Joseon Dynasty, there were armor as an armor that should be equipped when the people were convened in the military. Therefore, most of the time, the soldiers of the soldiers are restored to the armor on the left in the picture above.
In China , when the emperor's deputy dog left the West's expedition, he said that the soldiers of the West Station, who were salvation, did not die even if they wore a chain armor , and they were a horror object by dragging the soldiers who threw the no one. Conversely, this record can be assumed that China did not use chain armor in China. Since there is no recording of the chain armor, it seems that it has not brought the technology to make chain armor. Later, during the Tang Dynasty, there was a record that Dang Hyun -jong received a gift of a chain in the West Station. From this time, the chain armor appeared in earnest. Since then , chain armor has been used in earnest since the Ming Dynasty , and there is an illustration that is believed to be a pitching helmet in the Ming Dynasty's military encyclopedia.
It is not accurate to spread chain armor technology to Japan on the continent , but if you look at the artifacts excavated in Japan, it seems to have started to use chain armor since the Japanese expedition of Japan . However, Japan was not used as the main armor as the main armor, but was only used as an auxiliary tool for connecting the gaps of the auxiliary streams, such as Koto, which protects the arms or Snare, which protects the calf. Although it was called the Tadami -gu , it was just a tool for connecting the iron plate.
In the Edo period , Japan began to use chain armor as its main armor. It was easy to defend the knife effectively while being easily worn under the dress, so it was spotlighted by the samurai at the time.[5] In particular, in the end of the short part of the shogunate, the brushed armored was very popular. Shinseonjo also used chain armor.
The soldiers of the Dzungar Khanate, founded by Oirat nomads, are also said to have used chain armor. For reference, in Dzungar, professional craftsmen make chain armor, and if the chain armor is easily pierced by an arrow, the craftsman is said to be beheaded.
3.1.3. Middle East and Indian Peninsula
The Middle East and the Indian peninsula were very favored because of the advantage of being well ventilated in hot climates, and unlike Europe , which was stated in Flat & Rivethring, two rivets were reinforced, such as putting two rivets, and furthermore, they also used a lot of light gloves connecting chains and iron plates . The Ottoman Empire spent a continuous war with Europe and used dumb gaunllets to fight high -defense European armor , and it was particularly concerned in the Islamic world.
3.2. decline
Certainly, chain armor was well-ventilated, easy to put on and take off, and sometimes had a warming effect, but with the development of metal smelting technology, its limitations gradually appeared.
According to an anecdote left by the 12th century chronicler Gerald of Wales, there were frequent sporadic battles between the troops sent by the English Baron William de Braos and the Welsh along the border of Wales in the late 12th century, with Welsh longbowmen riding on horseback. It is said that when he shot the British Man-at-Arms, it went straight through the thigh and killed the horse. So, it was known that the arrow first pierced the man-at-arms' hauberk, pierced the cowl underneath it, pierced the leather clothing underneath and the man-at-arms' thigh, pierced the leather horse saddle, and became lodged in the horse's body. However, this is an exaggeration; the armor protecting the thighs at the time was chainmail, and it is confirmed that another arrow was lodged in the other leg.[6]
But the longbow is not the only thing to be feared on the battlefield. Weapons such as axes , spears , and halberds were powerful enough to deal with chain armor, and there are actually several examples of lower-class people using such weapons defeating knights. In the Battle of Kortriek in Flanders in 1302, the Flemish militia armed with weapons such as halberds and Gutentags defeated a large number of fully armed French regular troops and Flemish noble knights, and in 1315 in Morgarten, Swiss peasants He destroyed the Austrian Man at Arms with his halberd. A similar incident occurred in England in 1314 at the Battle of Banakburn, where Robert the Bruce's army, armed with early-type pikes, defeated the army of King Edward II of England.
There is no way to say that these events were important combats in the cross period of chain and sheet armor, but it is a little nonsense, tactics, fraud, and various constant factors to say that these battles have won and lost because of chain armor. This is an example of the strengthened infantry defeating the cavalry due to the economic growth of Europe, and it is difficult to see that the chain armor reveals the vulnerability. The series of decline and sheet metal has already been done slowly since the early 13th century. The transition period of transition period, which introduces a sheet metal armor , which is made of sheet metal to protect the limbs while using chain armor , is called transition period.
By the time when sheet metal armor has become commonplace, the manufacturing cost of the chain armor, which has a lot of hands, exceeds the price of sheet metal. In addition, the cost of the chain armor should be more tight in order to have the level of defense of sheet metal armor, but the tight chain armor does not have the advantages of the chain armor, which makes it impossible to switch, which is the advantage of the chain armor. Therefore, when sheet metal began to spread, the chain armor was inevitable. However, some of the high people were wearing it between sheet metal armor and thousand armor.
Like this, the plate armor was more priced and more defensive, but there was only the advantage of the chain armor, and even in the age of the plate armor,[7] Along with the boss of the armor, the history of the long chain armor also ends.
In the 17th century, chain mail armor was temporarily used in Eastern Europe to some extent. In order for plate armor to have meaningful defense against firearms, it had to be thicker than 3 mm, so lighter and more comfortable armor became popular among cavalrymen instead of heavy and expensive plate armor to stop bullets and swords. However, coats made of cowhide were popular in Western Europe. In Poland, middle-class conscript cavalrymen called Šepnicke (those wearing iron) wore suits of chain mail, and Russia followed the same trend.
However, this was only temporary, and it was withdrawn again in the late 17th century when infantry firepower increased. The reason for this is that when a person is shot, the chain of the chainmail shatters due to the impact of the bullet being lodged deep into the body like fragments . In addition, fragments of chains embedded in this way are often not 100% removed even through surgery, causing major problems. In the war you just saw, elite troops of the shogunate army wearing chain armor had a lower chance of survival if they were hit by a bullet than soldiers from the shogunate army who were not wearing armor. First of all, they would die from excessive bleeding on the field, and even if they lived in the field, they would suffer from tetanus due to chain fragments. I got caught and died. Moreover, even if he survived due to luck, he was unable to do any work requiring physical labor due to the chain fragments in his body, so he effectively lost his fighting ability. Thanks to this, the adage spread that if you want to survive on the battlefield, you should take off your chain mail.
Thus, after the late 17th century, even if the bullets were shot, the injuries caused by armor fragments were used with relatively rare sheet metal, and after that, the armor itself was no longer guaranteed defense. In the midst of this, India used the truthful armor as a real armor until the 19th century, and India had a transitional appearance of using modern army and artillery while still loving shields, swords and armor. This is because it was frequent to deal with pre -modern cold weapons.
However, it was still used to a small extent in situations other than the military or war. In particular, in Japan, during the late shogunate era, samurai armed with swords, like assassination squads, committed many murders as part of political terrorism, and chain mail played a major role in this case. No matter how dangerous it may be, it is impossible to wear heavy armor all day long, but it was able to be worn under clothes, was well ventilated, and sufficiently prevented sword attacks, so this saved the lives of many members of the shogunate. There are even cases where people were attacked more than 30 times but escaped safely![8]
According to an anecdote left by the 12th century chronicler Gerald of Wales, there were frequent sporadic battles between the troops sent by the English Baron William de Braos and the Welsh along the border of Wales in the late 12th century, with Welsh longbowmen riding on horseback. It is said that when he shot the British Man-at-Arms, it went straight through the thigh and killed the horse. No matter how dangerous it may be, it is impossible to wear heavy armor all day long, but it was able to be worn under clothes, was well ventilated, and sufficiently prevented sword attacks, so this saved the lives of many members of the shogunate. There are even cases where people were attacked more than 30 times but escaped safely![8]
3.3. hyundai
- For military use, the armor itself decline and lost its value, but it appeared as a protective suit of British troops during World War I.[9] The tanks in the form of a slow and heavy land warship at that time were very easy to be the target of the enemy, no matter what, the armored armor was poor, and the armor was often destroyed when the shells and rifle were accumulated. At that time, the crew was often injured by various metal fragments.
- There is also machine welded mail. It is woven with precision machines and is used for divers' protective clothing to prevent shark attacks, curtains to prevent debris at industrial sites, and blade defenses for meat processing. The rings are very small, but each one is welded, and it is characterized by being very strong because it is made of stainless or titanium steel. It is originally for industrial use, but because of its lightness, it is also used in non-historical Reinact systems. However, since it is usually at the level of industrial protective clothing, there is a problem that the wire is thin and the thickness is thin, so it is not used for human-to-human combat.
- It is used by police and security personnel for anti-knives .
- In fantasy and medieval movies, mail made of ABS plastic and painted with metallic spray is widely used. Real metal armor is heavy, difficult to maintain, and above all expensive, so it is only used in close-up scenes.[10]
As such, there is a demand for chain mail even in modern times. It is used as safety equipment in industrial settings, including reinactors , and even people buy it because it looks cool, but there are some differences from military use in the past.
In the Reinact series, the proportion of butted mail is very high. This is because chain armor is provided at a low price without any special strengthening treatment for those who no longer fight in chain armor but still want chain armor. However, the defense is hopeless as it has a low AR value and the ring drops significantly when cut sharply with a sword. It is at the human level. If made densely, the defense can block up to a sword thrust, but in this case, the big problem is that the weight is more than 5kg heavier than the historical weight. Currently, due to this problem, a company called Get Dress For Battle is producing butted mail made of spring steel with very strong tensile strength, but the manufacturer is still trying to refrain from fighting. Less dense than medieval armor.
Today, stainless steel chain mail has replaced the traditional steel chain mail. Originally, chain armor was an item that rusted quite easily due to the nature of being made of iron, and in order to wash it, it had to be washed using a more complicated and special washing method than cloth clothes. In the past, we filled a bucket full of sand with vinegar, rolled it around, ground it into sand, and washed it to remove rust. After washing, we had to oil it separately. As the rust falls, the chain gradually becomes more and more broken, making it unsuitable for combat in the long run, so it goes without saying that it needs to be replaced. However, modern people who buy armor as a hobby find it difficult to clean the armor individually when it rusts, so products made of stainless steel that are completely rust-free have been released. Modern chainmail is usually made of mild steel or iron, but stainless steel chainmail has the advantage of being stronger than this. So it is a bit more expensive.
4. form
The basics of chain armor are 'shirts made of chains'. The shirt made of chains, called hauberk by Europeans, was usually made long enough to reach the knee to protect the upper and lower body. split into The shortened one should be called haubergeon (hovergeon), but it is often used interchangeably with haubergeon and hovergeon.
In the 1100s, the arm (sleeve) part, which was made short and loose like a short-sleeved T-shirt, started to change into a form that fits the body well, extending to the wrist.
After 1175, the arm part did not end at the wrist, but a bag-shaped muffler with only the thumb sticking out was made into a chain to cover the hand. The mitten is not separated from the arm, but is connected, and since the palm side is not covered, it hangs from the wrist of the chain armor when removed. The muffler is worn only when combat is imminent. Later, a complete gauntlet form with leather gloves and chains sewn together was also devised.
Before 1250, the chain of hoods (covering the head) were attached to the hoverk, but from the 1250s, this part was made in a separate form. CoiF is a type of head covered in the Middle Ages, so it is correct to call the chain of chain. Both Hoburk and Kohif were tightly tightly tightened with strings or buckles so that there was no gap. Due to the flexibility of the chain, it was rare to protect the head only with mail core , and usually wrote a norman helm, a Great Helm, a Sugarov Helm, a kettle Hat, and a bash. Initially, the core and helmet were used, but the pitches do not need to cover the chain to the parts that the helmet can be covered with sufficient defenses, so it is changed to a Camail or Aventail method that puts the chain of the chain. The armor that covers the neck of the chain is called pixane.
In the early days of the age of chains, the legs were protected only with leather or linen pants, but after the 1100s, fully armed man-at-arms wore chain stocking-like leg protectors to protect their feet. These stockings are called chausses and are connected by a garter belt to a belt worn at the waist.[11] have been fixed Currently, the chain armor of the leg part is collectively called chaus, but there was a time when the thigh part was called chaussons, and the part from the knee down to chausses. It is difficult to see chaussons, as hauberks usually come to knee length, and it is common to see the two together as just chaussons.
Due to the structure, the weight is placed on the shoulders except for detachable parts such as the show and coif, but thanks to the flexibility of the shirt, the weight could be distributed to the pelvis by wearing a belt. The weight of the mail is the same as that of normal armor, and it is flexible and breathable, so it does not restrict movement, but carrying all the load on the shoulder increases the wearer's fatigue, so he had to wear a belt.
At first they did not wear anything outside the hauberk, but from around 1190 they began to wear a loose outer covering - the jupon or surcoat - outside the hauberk. The surcoat comes from the French meaning to wear over a white coat (cotta), and in fact, like most attire, it was not made exclusively for knights, but is a type of regular overcoat that was common in the Middle Ages. It usually had no or short sleeves, was in the form of an overcoat worn over other clothes, and was worn by both men and women. Like a chain, it is usually open at the front and back up to the crotch for use when riding a horse. The surcoat worn over chain armor originally served the role of a cloak that prevented rain or moisture from directly reaching the chain and the sun's rays from directly reaching the chain and heating it up, but later on, it also had its own emblem. This has been distorted and is known to be just clothes with emblems, but in fact, it is the opposite.
In Asia and other influenced areas, chain mail often had jacket-like openings on the chest or sides to make it easier to put on. (The East Asian chain armor and light armor above are typical forms.) Although this had the advantage of being easy to wear, it was prone to being vulnerable due to the open parts being pierced. So, I made the opening part overlap like the front of a jeogori. In this way, there is no opening, but the chain is doubled by the overlapping area, which increases the weight.
In Europe, it was mainly made in a form that completely covered the body, like a T-shirt. Therefore, it has good weight efficiency and is made to fit the body perfectly, so it is less likely to get in the way. However, when putting on and taking off, you have to raise your hands and bend down to wear a heavy, hard-to-stretch metal handle on your body, which is not good and uncomfortable. European-style chain armor changed in mobility and weight waste depending on whether one chain was added or less, so in the late Middle Ages, it was made more tailored to the individual body type.
Chains have excellent resistance to cuts, but because they are very flexible armor, they cannot absorb the impact force from stabbing attacks or blows, so injuries resulting from them cannot be avoided. However, because it was armor, it did not mean that it was helpless from being stabbed, as is commonly believed. Even though the chains were tightly made and well-made and well-maintained, it was difficult for stabbing attacks such as arrows to penetrate the gaps in the chains.[12] However, it is undoubtedly true that very sharp blades, such as swords or bodkin points that are designed for stabbing, are weak to stabbing because they are pushed into the gaps in the chain and become lodged.
Also, if you wear it on the bare skin, your skin is scratched by the chain, and if you hit a blunt attack, the broken chain digs into the flesh , and under the chain, you must wear leather clothes almost ( the way the Roman enemy of the classic era). I wore a chain on it. If you wear this, you can absorb the shock to some extent, but you can't get the attack completely by the body, so you have to block it with a shield. Originally, armor is to use less injuries.[13]
For medieval soldiers, chain armor was generally an excellent defensive tool and certainly a very good thing to have. If you wear a full set of chain armor, complete with a helmet and a shield, there is nothing to envy other armor. However, a full body set was very expensive, so only the wealthy men-at-arms and knights could afford a full set of chain mail, while poor lower-class warriors and ordinary soldiers had no choice but to wear only a top or plain quilted armor. There wasn't.
Compared to other metals such as swords or iron plates, the contact area with air per weight is extremely large, so rust is also very easy to rust. and trimmed However, if you do this, the rusty part is ground and the chain itself becomes smaller, so it is said that as it ages, it becomes thinner compared to the beginning, and its defense and durability decrease.
In the 1100s, the arm (sleeve) part, which was made short and loose like a short-sleeved T-shirt, started to change into a form that fits the body well, extending to the wrist.
After 1175, the arm part did not end at the wrist, but a bag-shaped muffler with only the thumb sticking out was made into a chain to cover the hand. Initially, the core and helmet were used, but the pitches do not need to cover the chain to the parts that the helmet can be covered with sufficient defenses, so it is changed to a Camail or Aventail method that puts the chain of the chain. The armor that covers the neck of the chain is called pixane.
In the early days of the age of chains, the legs were protected only with leather or linen pants, but after the 1100s, fully armed man-at-arms wore chain stocking-like leg protectors to protect their feet. These stockings are called chausses and are connected by a garter belt to a belt worn at the waist.[11] have been fixed Currently, the chain armor of the leg part is collectively called chaus, but there was a time when the thigh part was called chaussons, and the part from the knee down to chausses. It is difficult to see chaussons, as hauberks usually come to knee length, and it is common to see the two together as just chaussons.
Due to the structure, the weight is placed on the shoulders except for detachable parts such as the show and coif, but thanks to the flexibility of the shirt, the weight could be distributed to the pelvis by wearing a belt. The weight of the mail is the same as that of normal armor, and it is flexible and breathable, so it does not restrict movement, but carrying all the load on the shoulder increases the wearer's fatigue, so he had to wear a belt.
At first they did not wear anything outside the hauberk, but from around 1190 they began to wear a loose outer covering - the jupon or surcoat - outside the hauberk. The surcoat comes from the French meaning to wear over a white coat (cotta), and in fact, like most attire, it was not made exclusively for knights, but is a type of regular overcoat that was common in the Middle Ages. It usually had no or short sleeves, was in the form of an overcoat worn over other clothes, and was worn by both men and women. Like a chain, it is usually open at the front and back up to the crotch for use when riding a horse. The surcoat worn over chain armor originally served the role of a cloak that prevented rain or moisture from directly reaching the chain and the sun's rays from directly reaching the chain and heating it up, but later on, it also had its own emblem. This has been distorted and is known to be just clothes with emblems, but in fact, it is the opposite.
In Asia and other influenced areas, chain mail often had jacket-like openings on the chest or sides to make it easier to put on. (The East Asian chain armor and light armor above are typical forms.) Although this had the advantage of being easy to wear, it was prone to being vulnerable due to the open parts being pierced. So, I made the opening part overlap like the front of a jeogori. In this way, there is no opening, but the chain is doubled by the overlapping area, which increases the weight.
In Europe, it was mainly made in a form that completely covered the body, like a T-shirt. Therefore, it has good weight efficiency and is made to fit the body perfectly, so it is less likely to get in the way. However, when putting on and taking off, you have to raise your hands and bend down to wear a heavy, hard-to-stretch metal handle on your body, which is not good and uncomfortable. European-style chain armor changed in mobility and weight waste depending on whether one chain was added or less, so in the late Middle Ages, it was made more tailored to the individual body type.
Chains have excellent resistance to cuts, but because they are very flexible armor, they cannot absorb the impact force from stabbing attacks or blows, so injuries resulting from them cannot be avoided. However, because it was armor, it did not mean that it was helpless from being stabbed, as is commonly believed. Even though the chains were tightly made and well-made and well-maintained, it was difficult for stabbing attacks such as arrows to penetrate the gaps in the chains.[12] However, it is undoubtedly true that very sharp blades, such as swords or bodkin points that are designed for stabbing, are weak to stabbing because they are pushed into the gaps in the chain and become lodged.
Also, if you wear it on the bare skin, your skin is scratched by the chain, and if you hit a blunt attack, the broken chain digs into the flesh , and under the chain, you must wear leather clothes almost ( the way the Roman enemy of the classic era). I wore a chain on it. If you wear this, you can absorb the shock to some extent, but you can't get the attack completely by the body, so you have to block it with a shield. Originally, armor is to use less injuries.[13]
For medieval soldiers, chain armor was generally an excellent defensive tool and certainly a very good thing to have. If you wear a full set of chain armor, complete with a helmet and a shield, there is nothing to envy other armor. However, a full body set was very expensive, so only the wealthy men-at-arms and knights could afford a full set of chain mail, while poor lower-class warriors and ordinary soldiers had no choice but to wear only a top or plain quilted armor. There wasn't.
Compared to other metals such as swords or iron plates, the contact area with air per weight is extremely large, so rust is also very easy to rust. and trimmed However, if you do this, the rusty part is ground and the chain itself becomes smaller, so it is said that as it ages, it becomes thinner compared to the beginning, and its defense and durability decrease.
5. manufacturing
The key to manufacturing chain armor is the 'pattern' of how the chains are interwoven and how to control each ring.
The most common pattern in Europe is the 4-to-1 (also spelled 4-in-1), in which one ring connects to four neighboring rings. This method is the easiest and is the basis for chain armor.
If you increase the number of neighboring rings, it becomes more dense and strong. 6-in-1, 8-in-1, the shape is the same as 4-in-1, but double mail, which is a double layering method that overlaps two rings instead of one ring (king's mail) There is a way. However , in fact, 4-in-1s are almost dominant, as most of them increase in weight as they become denser .
Most rings were made of soft iron , so they were very soft, but some later rings were made of mild steel with a carbon content sufficient to allow heat treatment. When making a ring of a certain standard, it is made by winding a wire around a rod and cutting it into the same size, but just leaving both ends unconnected and facing each other is called a butted ring. This form was not used well because the ring was completely separated and torn apart when impacted, and the defense was poor, so it is usually used in imitations or, historically, it is considered to have been made for events.
There is also a flat ring made with a flat cross section like a washer rather than a ring shape with a round wire, but the flat ring can secure more structural strength than a round wire ring. However, in most cases, a riveted ring was used, which overlaps both ends, presses them flat, and then rivets to connect them. The rivet ring has been used since ancient times, as it was used from Lorica Hamata in the Roman era, and the weak point is the gap. It's the best way to prevent that from happening. Wedge riveted, which is not just a rivet, but a wedge-shaped riveting, was stronger than ordinary rivets, and for higher defense, two rivets were used to fix it, and staples (U-shaped nails) were rarely used.
And the ring with the best strength is a solid ring (or close ring) made by punching a hole in a steel plate or by welding both ends, but a solid ring has no broken parts in the first place, so it can produce the best strength, but how to connect rings because there is no In reality, the rivet ring and the solid ring were alternately combined row by row. Among the chain armor, this type is the strongest and can be called a proper combat chain armor.
The identity of "double mail", often referred to in historical sources, cannot be confirmed because the relics are currently unclear. It could be two layers of hauberk, two more rings (6-in-1), or two rings (8-in-1), or two rings stacked to create one. It could be an 8-in-2 pattern of king's mail (the expression "king's mail" is also a modern invention) that is made by inserting it into place. Anyway, in the document, there is a record that the double mail was as heavy as it was, so its defense was excellent, so it was not well penetrated by arrows and lances.
Usamah ibn Munqidh, an Islamic chronicler of the Crusades, tells of an Arab-Syrian warrior who struck down a Frankish knight with a joust, though the Arab warrior thought he must be dead . It is said that he was greatly surprised to see that the person he thought was dead came back alive and well. The Frankish knight was wearing double mail.
The Eastern Roman historian Anna Komnena describes a Norman knight wearing chainmail and carrying a kite shield as almost invulnerable, unable to be wounded by a bow drawn by a giant, let alone by a Turk . In battle, it was testified that the crossbows of the crusaders were a considerable threat to the archers, while the crusader infantrymen, dressed in thick cloth armor and chainmail, stood upright and guarded their ranks with dozens of arrows planted.
Chain armor as well as plate armor had proof, and various terms such as "haute cloueur", "demi-cloueur", "botte cassée", and "botte" were used to refer to it. The exact definition of these terms is vague, but they appear to refer to the type of attack or class of chain once mobilized in proof. Botte seems to refer to a sword strike, and haute or demi-clueurs seem to refer to one or two rivets that fasten each chain. Normal chains are fastened with one rivet, but there are also relics that use two rivets to obtain greater strength. do. "de toute botte" seems to express total proof against swords and axes, arrows and crossbows.
Since proof records distinguish between ordinary crossbows and high-power crossbows loaded with hoisters, it is estimated that protection against arrows would not have been impossible if chain armor was made of solid rings and rivet rings that had undergone heat treatment and were thick with double mail. . However, it is not clear whether the heat treatment would have benefited from arrow protection, but rather, there are experimental results that show that soft iron mail protects Bodkin arrows. # It is difficult to say that chain mail from many feeds was also heat treated. It is an area that requires investigation and research in many ways.
I don't know if it's because the issue of protective power of chain armor is out of the interest of historians, but the reality is that it is being experimented on mainly by amateurs. Due to cost issues, most of the chain armor used in Reinact are chain armor using butted rings that were not used for actual combat, and most of the defense tests on YouTube and other sites used them. Up to this video . Again, it is difficult to see it as a systematic experiment, but in the experiment using solid rings and rivet rings, you can see that it has quite a bit of protection against arrows and javelins. Therefore, it is necessary to approach the test and assertion about the protective power of the chain armor a little critically.
However, considering the maintenance problems of chain armor, it is clear that the variation in defense was quite severe depending on the economic situation of the wearer or the battlefield situation. As explained above, due to the large area exposed to the air, it rusted very easily, so it had to be removed frequently by rolling it in sand sprinkled with vinegar. If the ring became thin or torn and became unwearable, it was essential to replace the relevant part. However, this process costs money and time, so it would not be uncommon for the wearer to just wear it due to arrogant variables such as lack of money, the battlefield is humid, and the enemy is right around the corner, so there is no time to put it in the sand bucket, and arrows or blades are unlucky. There may have been occasional cases of piercing or cutting into vulnerable areas that could not be repaired. It was drilled into every historical document of the time. There is a reason for making conflicting statements, such as saying that it was thrown out.
6. Misunderstood Chainmail
19th century scholars such as Samuel Rusu Meyrick attempted to reproduce the form of armor literally... no, literally through medieval paintings and sculptures. So, instead of the usual European 4-in-1, I tried to reproduce the pattern shown in the picture. The studies of scholars in the 19th century are precious things that provide the basis for our research, but it was actually a bit unreasonable to reproduce the painting as it is. The problem is that most of the uniquely patterned armor shown in medieval paintings were not specially made, but were simply simplified/patterned chain drawings.
For example, many of the armor seen in Bayeux Tapestry are not just chainmail, but ringmail and banded mail, but everything that appears there is just chainmail.
In that way, the pattern of armor is wrong (probably not actually used).
For example, many of the armor seen in Bayeux Tapestry are not just chainmail, but ringmail and banded mail, but everything that appears there is just chainmail.
In that way, the pattern of armor is wrong (probably not actually used).
6.1. Banded mail
Some of the medieval illustrations are made from the portrayal of the pattern of the chain armor as if the band is connected as long as a band. Some scholars in the 19th century inferred the shape of a protective plate or ring that made up the armor, and in this form, in order to make the armor in this form, it was stitched in a leather cheon two layers to make a band -shaped pocket and made the chain ring in it. I tried to reproduce in the form of filling. Alternatively, the chain ring was made as long as it was a leaf. Even in the 19th century, I thought it was a real form, but later scholars concluded that this only interpreted medium -sized paintings.
Under the influence of D&D , this is also referred to as splinted mail, plated mail, plate and mail, which is armor made by interweaving chains between iron plates .
6.2. Ringmail
It is an armor similar to scale armor made by sewing rings onto cloth armor rather than connecting chains . This was also created by scholars of the Victorian era to interpret the painting as it is.
Unlike banded mail, ring mail did exist. The only problem is that it was not used as universally in medieval Europe as it appeared in paintings and sculptures. If you read the picture as it is, it should have been as common as chainmail, but no actual items from the Middle Ages have been found in Europe (except for other types described below), so it is thought to be a misrepresentation of ordinary mail.
Additionally, the term ring mail is also problematic. Ring and mail are tautologies, and scholars only allow the expression mail to be a variation of chain mail or a partial use of mail. This armor is more of a relative of scale armor, being less dense and more flexible than scale armor. Therefore, it is correct to call it ring armor rather than ring mail.
First of all, ring armor type armor existed in both the East and Europe. In records from the 16th and 17th centuries, there are records and relics of armor in the form of a doublet or coat with a ring sewn on it, called eyelet doublet or eyelet coat, and in Germany by the name "Schiessjoppe". This can certainly be called ring armor, but unfortunately it is from the early modern era, not the medieval era. Among the Frankish items from the Carolingian period, rings or disks were added to regular cloth clothing to reinforce them and were used as combat uniforms (called broigne maclée in French). There is a subtle feeling as to whether this should be considered the same as ring armor made by interpreting pictures from historical sources. There is.
6.3. etc
In addition, Meyrick presented his opinion by naming the patterns he had experimented with in the course of his research as tegulated mail, masked mail, trelliced mail, etc. Scholars such as John Hewitt and Charles Ffoulkes considered Meyrick's opinion to be incorrect, and in the 20th century Enter and finally conclude that Claude Blaire is wrong, and this is accepted as a universal theory in the sword and armor world. However, because the opinions of later scholars such as Charles Henry Ashdown and Eugène Emmanuel Viollet-le-duc, who supported Myric's theory, were adopted in fantasy role-playing games, non-existent armor and incorrect terms spread to the subculture world.
The reason banded mail and ring mail became known in the subculture was because they appeared as armor in Dungeons & Dragons. Moreover, it was Dandy's original sin to suddenly add the word 'mail' to any piece of armor. Scale mail, banded mail, ring mail, etc. For this reason, there was discussion about this in Dragon Magazine during the AD&D era. Well, in the end, the people who were arguing said, “It’s possible because it’s fantasy.” And it was double-crossed, and in the end, after the fourth round, it was merged into a chain plate.
However, this is not only a problem for the dandy who brought it up, but the person who believes the game book straight away and picks it up without cross-checking is also a fool. Moreover, the banded mail shown in Dandy is not actually a chain ring threaded like a coin, but rather a line of small pieces of armor on a thin chain on padding, connected like a belt and reinforced to form an armor, similar to splint mail or lorica. It is expressed in a strange and soft way that reminds me of Segmentata.

7. in the media
Chain armor appears more frequently than in other cultures in Western countries, but it is less recognized than plate armor , so it does not appear often. Because plate armor is overwhelming in terms of sharpness. That's why it's often worn by extras or supporting actors.
However, due to the perception that iron armor looks strong because it is made of iron armor and is lighter and easier to move than plate armor, the image of expeditions or adventurers who are more agile and need to move more often than warriors or gladiators who enjoy fighting with onslaught comes to mind, and light armor and Due to the unique image of heavy armor, there are a small number of enthusiasts. It's a bonus to enjoy the sound effect of chains hitting each other every time you move. Fans of the latter are fans of RPGs with light or heavy armor concepts, or are particularly influenced by the Lord of the Rings live-action film series .
However, if the protagonist is a Nordic family such as the Viking and Norman, the macho castle is emphasized, or if the crusades are the case of the crusades , or if the crusades are the crusades, or in the case of the crusades, the activity is emphasized in the case of a mathematical or an adventurer. Many cases are worn as inner armors.[14] Other than that, it appears only in works faithful to representation. The movie Kingdom of Heaven is a typical.[15]
However, in the field of cartoon animation, it is not just looked down on, but a cesspool. The reason why it is looked down on the most is because it is very difficult to draw the details of numerous iron rings and chains to match the body, and even if the skills are excellent, the amount of work increases enormously, so it is very low in cost-effectiveness. The reason is similar to the fact that there are relatively few older sister characters wearing fishnet stockings, even though there is a lot of demand even in mulberry circles . So, there are two cases that appear in comics, but I omit as much detail as possible.[16] A dramatized, live-action war cartoon set in the background of the Crusades , in which background extras or chainmail inevitably appear . In the latter case, the work itself is precious, but it guarantees that the artist's skills exceed an extremely outstanding level. If you try to draw something yourself, even if it is simple, you will immediately realize how cumbersome and time-consuming it is.[17] Whether it's fishnet stockings or chainmail, it will be difficult to increase the number of drawings drawn in the future unless AI can draw them naturally and act as an assistant.
However, it is quite good for the film world, where there is no difference in the effort of making props, or the game world, where there is no difference in the amount of texture work . The main characters in the Lord of the Rings live-action film series and the Hobbit live-action film series are all wearing chain mail or plate armor, although they are hard to see because they are wearing clothes. Plus, somehow, the closer you get to extra, the better the armor becomes.[18] Rather, the soldiers of Gondor, such as Haldir, wear plate metal, and the armor of the villains , the Urukhai and battle trolls, is rather heavily armored, although it is rough. Perhaps this difference was decided because the protagonist and his party were going on a journey, and chain armor had a stronger 'image' of being more active and simple than plate armor.[19] However, even though it is a chain, for example, the armor worn by Frodo is made of mithril chain armor, so it cannot be said to be worse than plate.[20] Thanks to that strong armor, he was saved from a potentially fatal attack.[21]
In video and games, a high probability of missing chuts (hand protection) and bashinet (helmet) are missing. In other words, it is protecting the whole body by e -mail and exposes the most damaged fingers and heads to danger. If you don't wear a helmet, you'll see the character's face, so it's easy to know who is. In reality, however, Salena Bashinet was worn on the mail cowife to protect the head from the attack. In the case of fingers, it is often exposed to the actual painting of the Middle Ages, which is probably to use the swordsmanship more flexibly. Anyway, the long sword has a cross guard that protects the hands, so if the wearer has learned the swordsman properly, he does not need a hand protection.
In the comedy film Monty Python and the Holy Grail, the Knights of the Round Table all wear chain mail, but only the main character, King Arthur ( Graham Chapman ), wears real chain mail, while the other characters all wear fake chain mail. It is said to have been made by painting knitted clothes silver, but in fact, it is often used as a substitute for low-budget movies, plays, or low-cost cosplay among Westerners. Also, where this method is surprisingly useful is in the production of 1/6 figures such as Hot Toys . Making chain armor that can be worn on a figure by weaving real metal chains is not only difficult, time consuming, and expensive, but even if it is made with such painstaking effort, the fit is not very good and there is no space to put it on top, so even high-end figures are made of silver net-shaped fabric such as silver thread scrubber.
In Korean and Chinese historical dramas, there are almost no appearances due to the fact that they are just cloth clothes, head armor or armor, and plate armor wearing a mask of armor. The only case where the main character wears it is Empress Dowager Tianchu (drama) . Interestingly, this chain armor prop is said to have been made by the production team of The Lord of the Rings.
However , it appears very often, especially in Western video games . Most of them go through before going through plate armor, but taking advantage of the fact that they are more active than plate armor (actually, chain armor is also very heavy), characters or classes that mainly wear chains appear.
In The Legend of Zelda: Twilight Princess and The Legend of Zelda: Skyward Sword, Link appears wearing a suit.
Waldoche Law called it a chain letter . Disaster caused by interpreting Mail as a letter, not armor. In particular, the interpretation of Elven chain mail as Eleven chain mail is often cited as Waldoche's hidden beauty.
It is called mail in English as one of the four armor classifications of World of Warcraft , cloth-leather-chain-plate classification. There are hunter , shaman , and originator classes to wear . However, it is considered to be one of the most neglected types of armor, and only two of them could be worn before the dragonflight, and throughout the expansion pack, it is notorious for its really ugly appearance except for a few sets that are prone to drought. Because the above-mentioned 'method of applying a chain texture on the skin' is used unnecessarily, the chain pattern is worn and a particularly ridiculous helmet or decorative shoulder armor is added on top of it. Most of them. At least, shamans are usually designed with natural phenomena as the theme, so they are good enough to look at, but the hunter tea set has only a handful of well-received designs. Because of this, there are very few races in Allah & Horde that can pull off this kind of chain look well.
In Mount & Blade , a game that embodies medieval warfare, all armors with moderate defense are these chain-type armor. Even when equipped with this, it provides considerable defense against slashing weapons. The weight is set lower than the plate, so the activity is good, and compared to the full plate, which is only one type in the vanilla version, if you combine pure chains or chains reinforced with metal plates, you have a wide range of choices as you like. However, it is not very effective against blunt weapons or stabbing weapons, and blunt weapons have low basic attack power and cannot be blocked well even with full plates, so they are not really good against arrows and javelins. In fact, in this game, there are some people who don't like plate armor because it's not cool. Because it's so clunky. The difference in equipment is covered to some extent through shields, skills, and troops. However, the plate's defense is superior, so it is almost invincible unless it is an Arbalest series or a low difficulty level. However, since Bannerlord , the sequel , has a more historical background, plate armor is removed from the top armor position.[22] Chain armor with densely woven lamellas or iron pieces added in some cultures serves as the top level of armor.
Even in the light novel The Shield Hero Success Story, it appears as equipment in the beginning of the main character, Naofumi Iwatani , but a certain incident[23] Since then, her chain armor has gone unnoticed. Instead, he prefers custom armor that strengthens his defense by partially adding iron plates to the base of his leather armor.
The goddess , the main character of Goblin Slayer, followed Goblin Slayer's advice and wore chain armor under her clothes after being severely treated by a goblin at the beginning of Volume 1. Goblin Slayer wears leather armor on top of chain mail .
Monster hunters and witchers in The Witcher series often wear chain armor as their attire.
In the world of Magnus Chase and the Gods of Asgard, Thor comes out wearing chain armor.
Fabian Kristianen from author Min-Hee Jeon's novel The Stone of the Years asks the blacksmith to hold the handle of the Dawning Sword in order to lower the price of the Chain Armor. Of course, the Dawn sword felt hot except for Fabian, so the blacksmith casually reduced the price to Fabian who grabbed the Dawn sword.

In Mount & Blade , a game that embodies medieval warfare, all armors with moderate defense are these chain-type armor. Even when equipped with this, it provides considerable defense against slashing weapons. The weight is set lower than the plate, so the activity is good, and compared to the full plate, which is only one type in the vanilla version, if you combine pure chains or chains reinforced with metal plates, you have a wide range of choices as you like. However, it is not very effective against blunt weapons or stabbing weapons, and blunt weapons have low basic attack power and cannot be blocked well even with full plates, so they are not really good against arrows and javelins. In fact, in this game, there are some people who don't like plate armor because it's not cool. Because it's so clunky. The difference in equipment is covered to some extent through shields, skills, and troops. However, the plate's defense is superior, so it is almost invincible unless it is an Arbalest series or a low difficulty level. However, since Bannerlord , the sequel , has a more historical background, plate armor is removed from the top armor position.[22] Chain armor with densely woven lamellas or iron pieces added in some cultures serves as the top level of armor.
Even in the light novel The Shield Hero Success Story, it appears as equipment in the beginning of the main character, Naofumi Iwatani , but a certain incident[23] Since then, her chain armor has gone unnoticed. Instead, he prefers custom armor that strengthens his defense by partially adding iron plates to the base of his leather armor.
The goddess , the main character of Goblin Slayer, followed Goblin Slayer's advice and wore chain armor under her clothes after being severely treated by a goblin at the beginning of Volume 1. Goblin Slayer wears leather armor on top of chain mail .
Monster hunters and witchers in The Witcher series often wear chain armor as their attire.
In the world of Magnus Chase and the Gods of Asgard, Thor comes out wearing chain armor.
Fabian Kristianen from author Min-Hee Jeon's novel The Stone of the Years asks the blacksmith to hold the handle of the Dawning Sword in order to lower the price of the Chain Armor. Of course, the Dawn sword felt hot except for Fabian, so the blacksmith casually reduced the price to Fabian who grabbed the Dawn sword.
The second volume of the Vikings, a novel by British author and explorer Tim Severin, contains a brief debate among Vikings over chain mail.
The handsome Danish man boldly questioned the instructor's order to repeat the training from the beginning wearing the Birni, a hot and heavy chain mail. The instructor smiled eerily. He instructed us to lean the shield against a wooden frame and place the pig carcass behind it. Then he went into the arsenal, took out a javelin, took aim and threw the first javelin twenty paces away. Then the metal spear blade pierced the shield cleanly and pierced the pig's carcass a span of length. "Now you should know why you have to train while wearing Birni, Odin's net." the instructor barked.Vikings Volume 2 / Written by Tim Severin / Translated by Wonkyung Lee / 208 pages
It also appears in Minecraft . The defense power is stronger than that of leather sets and weaker than that of iron armor. It cannot be crafted and can only be obtained by trading with a villager or by killing zombies or skeletons that are created while wearing this on normal or higher difficulty levels. It is used as a fashion item because of its unique rarity and the ability to show through the skin.
